ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese steamed chicken with kaffir lime leaves. FOODON_03302050 (chicken food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for steamed chicken with kaffir lime leaves in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Cooked chicken) is the closest available term. No specific concept exists for this Vietnamese preparation.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately half a chicken or 300 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gà hấp lá chanh consists of approximately 300 g of steamed chicken pieces, often presented with dipping sauce and steamed rice on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted — adapted to steamed preparation; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database steamed chicken entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macro values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken meat only (FDC ID 171477), which closely approximates steamed chicken without skin. Values are cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo steamed chicken entries. Steaming retains more moisture than roasting, so fat values are conservatively aligned to the lower end of the USDA range. Kaffir lime leaves and aromatics contribute negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. Sodium is estimated to reflect light seasoning with fish sauce as commonly prepared.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — protein-dominant meat dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure protein and fat foods such as plain cooked chicken have a glycemic index of effectively 0 because they contain negligible digestible carbohydrate. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently classify protein-dominant meat dishes as GI = 0 (non-glycemic). Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for non-glycemic foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is set to low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is negligible (0.5 g per 100 g × 300 g serving = 1.5 g total carbohydrate, GL rounds to 0.0). Documented: glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this dish contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or dipping sauce on the side, as a 300 g serving may contribute approximately 840 mg sodium, representing a significant portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gà hấp lá chanh is an excellent protein source with negligible carbohydrate (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a low-GI carbohydrate side and consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate saturated fat; steaming method avoids added oils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steaming without added oil keeps saturated fat at approximately 1.8 g per 100 g, making this dish a heart-friendly protein choice; however, patients with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake from accompanying sauces and limit portion size to manage overall dietary fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gà hấp lá chanh (Vietnamese: Gà hấp lá chanh) is a traditional Vietnamese steamed chicken dish distinguished by the aromatic infusion of kaffir lime leaves (lá chanh), lemongrass, and ginger. The chicken — typically a whole bird or bone-in pieces — is marinated with fish sauce, salt, and pepper before being steamed over high heat until fully cooked, allowing the citrus-floral fragrance of the lime leaves to permeate the meat. The result is tender, juicy chicken with a delicate herbal aroma and a clean, lightly savoury flavour profile that is markedly different from roasted or fried preparations. The dish is commonly served with steamed jasmine rice, a wedge of fresh lime, and a dipping sauce of salt, pepper, and lime juice or a light fish sauce mixture. In Singapore, it is offered at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and is priced at the premium end of the menu, reflecting the quality of ingredients and preparation. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 25 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrate (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (approximately 6.5 g per 100 g), with the steaming method avoiding the addition of cooking oils. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, and is a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health when sodium from dipping sauces is controlled. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Gà hấp lá chanh was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken (meat only, FDC ID 171477) and cross-referenced with HPB steamed chicken entries. The adaptation is well-supported for a steamed, minimally seasoned chicken dish, but the specific contribution of kaffir lime leaves and fish sauce seasoning introduces minor uncertainty in sodium and micronutrient values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 (chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) adjusted for steaming preparation and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed chicken data. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero — steamed chicken with no added hydrogenated fats contains no industrially produced trans fats per USDA data. 3. sugar_g is 0.0; confirmed zero — no added sugars and negligible intrinsic sugars in plain steamed chicken meat per USDA SR Legacy. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero — pure meat dish with no plant fibre-contributing ingredients at nutritionally significant quantities per USDA SR Legacy. 5. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this dish contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g); gi_category low per convention for protein-dominant meat dishes. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 (1.5 g total carbohydrate per 300 g serving yields GL < 1, rounded to 0.0). 6. Sodium of 280 mg per 100 g is an estimate reflecting light fish sauce seasoning as typically prepared; plain steamed chicken without seasoning would be approximately 75–90 mg per 100 g per USDA data. 7. The cuisine field is set to Vietnamese reflecting the dish's origin, though the input record listed Singapore as cuisine (reflecting the GrabFood Singapore sourcing context). 8. Price of SGD 45.00 is preserved from the input record as contextual metadata but is not used in nutritional scoring. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 and are not -1 as the source reports these values for cooked chicken meat.
